content.problemStatement:
Alice and Bob are studying for their class test together. The topic of the test is **Prime Numbers**. The preparation is getting too boring for their liking. To make it interesting, they turn it into a game. The winner will get an ice-cream treat from the other.

The game is called **Count K-Primes**. A number is a KKK-prime if it has exactly KKK **distinct** prime factors. The game is quite simple. Alice will give three numbers AAA, BBB & KKK to Bob. Bob needs to tell Alice the number of KKK-prime numbers between AAA & BBB (both inclusive). If Bob gives the correct answer, he gets a point. If not, Alice gets a point. They play this game TTT times.

Bob hasn't prepared so well. But he really wants to win the game. He wants you to tell him the correct answer.
content.inputFormat:
First line of input contains a single integer TTT, the number of times they play.
Each game is described in a single line containing the three numbers AAA,BBB & KKK.
content.outputFormat: For each game, output on a separate line the number of KKK-primes between AAA & BBB.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: The range includes 444 integers {2,3,4,5}\{2, 3, 4,5 \}{2,3,4,5}. We need to find the number of integers in this range having only 111 distinct prime factor.

For 222, since it is prime, it has 111 distinct prime factor.
For 333, since it is prime, it has 111 distinct prime factor.
For 444, we can write it as 2×22\times 22×2. Thus, it has 111 distinct prime factor which is 222.
For 555, since it is prime, it has 111 distinct prime factor.

Thus, all 444 integers in the given range are 111-primes.
Test case 222: The only K-primes in the given range are 666 and 101010. This is because 6=2×36 = 2\times 36=2×3 and 10=2×510 = 2\times 510=2×5. Thus both these integers have 222 distinct prime factors.
Test case 333: The only K-primes in the given range are 141414 and 151515. This is because 14=2×714 = 2\times 714=2×7 and 15=3×515 = 3\times 515=3×5. Thus both these integers have 222 distinct prime factors.
Test case 444: There are no K-primes in the given range.
